text.title: Load Balancing Algorithms Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed in real load-balancer deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: One backend in a round-robin pool shows 90% CPU while peers idle at 20%. p99 latency degrades during business hours; restarting the hot backend "fixes it" for a few hours.
antipattern.root-cause: Round-robin distributes by new-connection count, not work cost. The workload contains long-running endpoints (uploads, exports) that pin sessions to a server.
antipattern.fix: Switch to `leastconn`. No code change required; the hot server stops receiving new connections until it drains. Measure: hot-spot CPU should converge with peers within minutes.

antipattern.symptom: Corporate customers report all their employees route to the same backend; rolling restarts of that one backend visibly disrupt them while other backends stay idle.
antipattern.root-cause: `ip_hash` is configured for "sticky sessions"; the customer's 5000 users share one NAT egress IP.
antipattern.fix: Replace `ip_hash` with cookie-based persistence (`sticky cookie SRVID insert indirect nocache` in HAProxy; `sticky_cookie` in Nginx Plus or app-level sticky cookies in OSS Nginx). Each real user gets their own affinity.

antipattern.symptom: Two new larger nodes added to the pool; cluster throughput barely improves. The small node still saturates first under load tests.
antipattern.root-cause: Pool uses plain `leastconn` (or RR) with no `weight=` directives; the small node receives equal connections to the large ones and remains the bottleneck.
antipattern.fix: Set `weight` proportional to capacity (`server large1 ... weight=100; server small1 ... weight=50`). Recheck under the same load test — small node's queue should now drain in step with large ones.
